caption: Fig. 1: Graphical representation of the aggregate computing system model.
description2: A graphical representation of the aggregate computing system model is plotted.
description3: Three-panel plot displays a network diagram and three sequential stages, labeled "Sense," "Compute," and "Interact," connected by a horizontal arrow indicating a computational round.
questions: What is the overall graphical representation of this aggregate computing system?
How does the model depict the progression through the system's stages?

caption: Fig. 2: Architecture of Phyelds. Modules (components) contain classes (rect- angles), submodules (dashed borders), and functions (ovals). Arrows indicate dependencies.
description2: The architecture of Phyelds is composed of modules containing classes, submodules with dashed borders, and functions represented by ovals, connected by arrows indicating dependencies.
description3: Two-panel architecture diagram displays modules, submodules, classes, and functions connected by arrows indicating dependencies.
questions: What are the main modules and components that make up Phyelds' architecture?
How do the modules and components relate to each other within the system?

caption: Fig. 3: Examples of common aggregate programming patterns implemented in Phyelds. On the left, a channel is constructed between a source device (bottom left) and a target device (top right). The channel color is yellow. On the right, devices are partitioned into coordination regions (different colors).
description2: The figure illustrates examples of common aggregate programming patterns implemented in Phyelds, featuring a yellow channel construction between devices on the left and device partitioning into colored coordination regions on the right.
description3: Two-panel plot displays a channel connecting devices on the left, and devices partitioned into colored coordination regions on the right.
questions: What are some common programming patterns demonstrated using these device arrangements?
How are devices organized and connected in these different visual representations?

caption: Fig. 4: Vicsek flocking simulation in VMAS using Phyelds. Agents (cirlces) align their velocities over time (left to right), exhibiting coherent collective motion.
description2: A line chart plots the alignment of agents' velocities over time, with circles representing individual agents moving coherently from left to right.
description3: Three-panel plot displays circular agents with velocity vectors, showing alignment and coherent collective motion from left to right.
questions: How does the behavior of individual agents change over the course of the simulation?
What is the overall trend in the collective motion of the agents shown?
